1: “That same day (bayyôm hahûʾ) King Ahasuerus gave Queen Esther the estate of Haman.” The confiscation of a condemned official’s property by the crown is standard Persian legal procedure, attested in the Fortification Tablets from Persepolis. Esther receives Haman’s estate — the material reversal accompanies the political one. Mordecai’s appearance before the king marks his formal introduction to the court; Esther reveals his relationship to her, integrating the two halves of the story that had been kept separate.
2: The king removes the signet ring from Haman’s hand (having recovered it — Haman had presumably been wearing it as the authorized agent of the decree) and gives it to Mordecai. The signet ring is the instrument of legal authority in the Persian system: whatever it seals is the king’s command. The ring that authorized the genocide of the Jews is now in the hands of a Jew. Esther appoints Mordecai over Haman’s estate — the administration of the condemned man’s household passes to his intended victim.
3: Esther’s second intercession for her people requires approaching the king again, falling at his feet and weeping. The combination of physical prostration (tippōl lipnê raglāyw), weeping (wattēḇḵ), and supplication (wattitḥannen) is the full posture of covenant pleading, paralleling Moses’ intercession after the golden calf (Exodus 32:11-13) and David’s prayer in Psalm 51. The “evil scheme of Haman the Agagite and his plot which he had devised against the Jews” is identified by name even after Haman’s execution — the decree itself is still active and must be addressed.
4: The king again extends the golden scepter — the third use of this gesture in the book (Esther 5:2; earlier in Esther 8). The first was survival (Esther 5:2); the second was petition (Esther 5:3); the third is renewal of the right to speak. Esther rises from prostration to standing — the movement from suppliant to advocate. The throne-room scene is repeated with slightly different stakes: now it is not about personal survival but about the survival of a people.
5: “If it pleases the king, and if I have found favour in his sight, and if the matter seems right to the king, and if I am pleasing in his eyes” — Esther uses four conditional clauses of deference (ʾim-ṭôḇ ʿal-hammelek... wᵉʾim-māṣāʾṯî ḥēn... wᵉkāšēr... wᵉṭôḇāh ʾᵃnî) before making her request. The accumulation is a rhetorical technique of maximizing the appearance of submission to increase the likelihood of a favorable response. Persian court petitions required elaborate formulaic deference.
6: “For how can I endure to see the disaster that will come upon my people? How can I bear to see the destruction of my kindred?” Esther’s emotional appeal is not rhetorical but existential: she is asking the king to understand the unbearability of watching one’s people destroyed. The double question (ʾêḵāh ʾûḵal wᵉrāʾîṯî) mirrors the lament form of Lamentations and Psalms — the language of covenant grief. Her request is grounded not in legal argument but in the king’s capacity for empathy with her distress.
7: The king’s response is not a new decree but an authorization: he reminds Esther and Mordecai that he has already given Haman’s estate to Esther and hanged Haman, then charges them: “Now write on behalf of the Jews as you see fit, in the king’s name, and seal it with the king’s signet ring.” The delegation of legislative authority to Esther and Mordecai is remarkable — the king essentially cedes the composition of the counter-decree to its intended beneficiaries, a level of trust that reflects Mordecai’s new position.
8: “For no decree written in the king’s name and sealed with the king’s ring can be revoked” — the irrevocability of Persian royal decrees is a structural constraint that runs through both the book of Esther and Daniel (Daniel 6:8, 12, 15). The Medes-and-Persians law principle means that the original genocidal decree cannot be cancelled; it can only be countermanded by an equally authoritative decree permitting the Jews to defend themselves. The legal impossibility of revocation is the narrative tension that makes the counter-decree necessary rather than optional.
9: The scribes are summoned on the twenty-third day of the third month — Sivan — approximately nine months before the thirteenth of Adar (Esther 9:1). This interval is intentional: the Jews must have time to organize, arm, and communicate the counter-decree across all 127 provinces. The decree is issued in every language — “according to the script of every province and the language of every people, and also to the Jews in their script and language” — an administrative detail that emphasizes the Persian empire’s multilingual bureaucracy and the Jews’ distinct identity within it.
10: Hārākkûš ḥᵃmûšîm — the horses are described as “bred from the royal stud” (or “royal relay horses”); the exact meaning is debated, but the emphasis is on speed. The royal courier system (ʾāḥaštᵉrānîm) was the Persian imperial postal service, the fastest communication network in the ancient world; Herodotus describes it admiringly. The counter-decree travels with maximum urgency, mirroring and countering the urgency of the original decree in Esther 3:15.
11: The counter-decree grants the Jews “the right to assemble and defend their lives” — lᵉhiqqāhēl ûlᵉʿamōḏ ʿal-napšām — and to destroy, kill, and annihilate (lᵉhašmîḏ lahᵃrōḡ ûlᵉʾabbēḏ) any armed force that attacks them, including women and children, and to plunder their property. The permission to take plunder mirrors what the original decree authorized against the Jews (Esther 3:13) — the counter-decree is a structural mirror image of the original. Notably, the Jews will not take the plunder (Esther 9:10, 15, 16), distinguishing their warfare from that of their attackers.
12: The timing — the thirteenth day of Adar — is the date Haman had selected by pur (lot) in Esther 3:7. The counter-decree specifies the same date as the original; rather than postponing the crisis, it transforms it on the same day into a test of who will attack and who will defend. The providence of pur-reversal reaches its logical conclusion: the day of death becomes the day of deliverance.
13: “A copy of the document was to be issued as law (dāṯ) in every province, publicly announced to all peoples, so that the Jews would be ready on that day to avenge themselves on their enemies.” The word dāṯ is a Persian loanword (cognate with Avestan dāta, law/decree) used throughout Esther and Daniel for imperial legal pronouncements. Its use here emphasizes that the counter-decree has the same legal force as the original — not a special exception but a standing royal law.
14: The couriers depart “urgently, pressed on by the king’s command” — the same urgency as 3:15 where the first decree was dispatched. The decree arrives in Susa simultaneously with its proclamation, meaning the capital and the provinces receive word together. The deliberate mirroring of the original dispatch process signals that this is a legally equivalent counter-decree, not merely a royal wish.
15: Mordecai leaves the king’s presence wearing lᵉḇûš malkûṯ tᵉkelēṯ wāḥûr — royal garments of blue and white — with a large golden crown (ʿᵃṭeret zāhāḇ gᵉḏôlāh) and a fine linen cloak (taʿᵃṭep bûṣ wᵉʾargāmān). Blue and white are Persian royal colors; the combination of crown, robe, and cloak represents full royal investiture. Mordecai in chapter 4 wore sackcloth; Mordecai in chapter 8 wears the full apparatus of royal authority. The city of Susa shouts in joy — the same city that was in confusion after the first decree (Esther 3:15) now rejoices.
16: Layyᵉhûḏîm hāyᵉṯāh ʾôrāh wᵉśimḥāh wᵉśāśōn wîqār — “For the Jews there was light and gladness, joy and honor.” The four-word formula of complete well-being is a stylistic marker of total reversal; the tetrad matches the fourfold negation implicit in Esther 4:1-3 (darkness, mourning, weeping, shame). In the liturgy of Havdalah (the Jewish ceremony separating Sabbath from the week), this verse is traditionally quoted — suggesting its early reception as a declaration of salvific reversal.
17: Miṯyahadîm — “declared themselves Jews” — is a unique denominalization, the first attested use of a verb meaning “to become/act as a Jew.” The motivation is pāḥaḏ hayyᵉhûḏîm nāpal ʿᵃlêhem — “the fear of the Jews fell upon them” — which echoes the Holy War formula of divine terror preceding Israel’s victories (cf. Exodus 15:16; Deuteronomy 11:25). Whether these conversions were sincere or opportunistic, the narrator notes them without judgment; the theological resonance with Ruth’s covenant loyalty and the inclusion of foreigners under YHWH’s protection is present in the background.
